sentences.text: In Mauthausen, we tried to go forward.
That-- the day would have been approximately the last few days of January.
We left Auschwitz on the 18th.
The last hot meal we had on the 17th.
And now it was approximately the 30th or 31st of January, 1945.
So we marched forward, the three of us, with our arm bands, official-- we are officials.
We looked like camp officials.
Actually, we were not.
But we-- in Auschwitz, we used to be.
But in Mauthausen, we were just ordinary nothing.
So we went forward, past many dead people, and past a few alive people.
And we jumped-- we went about 100 meters forward, approaching the wash house, the-- disinfect-- disinfection room, it was called.
And then we-- as we got more and more forward, it was harder and harder to go, because the stronger people, by natural selection-- they were at the front already to try to get in.
The weaker ones-- the weaker you got, the more the back you were.
So then they still let us through, a bit-- officials, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
And-- but eventually, the stage was reached when the people got so strong [LAUGHS] around us, they were smarter than us.
They said, all right, so what you-- why should I-- I don't make place for you.
You just wait in your line.
And if you don't like it, I punch you in the-- in the nose, you know.
But by that time, the stronger is the winner, you know?
So I said, OK, all right, OK, OK, cool down, all right, yeah.
We won't push past you.
We realize that he's stronger than us.
So we stayed there.
That was around 5:00 PM.

And by about 8:00 PM or 9:00 PM, under four hours standing in the bitter cold, we got into the-- into the disinfection room, which was just a concrete-- concrete floor, basically, with six water, you know, things running.
And take everything off, of course, usually.
Just-- just before I got to the room, to our amazement, we could see the people who come out on the other side of the-- of the shower room-- naked, of course.
They come out naked, and hot-- hot, steamy bodies.
And there was a group of people who were standing there for hours and hours outside, naked, with their hot bodies.
And gradually, they fell down, and froze to death.
And so we thought this-- this looks pretty grim, because we get our shower, then we have to stand outside in minus 20 with-- with steaming hot bodies.
That doesn't look too good.
So anyhow, we stepped into the shower, took everything off.
And as my bad luck would have it, I stepped on a piece of soap, which was lying there underneath.
I didn't see it.
I stepped on it.
And I fell flat on my back.
I fell down.
The SS man who stands there supervising-- it's a terrible job, to supervising dozens of people showering for hours and hours.
He just stepped there.
He said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Are you drunk, you bloody Jew?
Because I looked like I'm drunk.
You know, I fell over in the shower.
So he got his big stick, and he hit-- hit one into my face, and broke my nose here, this nose.
And he-- a couple more times, he hit over my head, which fractured a few nerves in my eye that I got blind because of that.
And so he was ready to kill me, when-- oh, so you are drunk?
So you're not willing to get up, are you?
And then two of my friends who were still around us and-- and they helped me up.
Said, oh, yes, he's-- he's not drunk.
He just slips on a soap.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
Don't talk so much, just get ready with your shower.
We had about 30 seconds in the shower, just a bit of water, cleaned a bit of mud away, and out on the other side.
They carried me out, because I-- I was still dazed from the two big blows on my face.
They carried me out.
And then we had to stand outside for half an hour.
And then, eventually, we got underpants and singlets.
And then we had to go outside.
So we are not stark naked.
We had underpants and singlet.
I remember that.
And we only had to wait until our group is filled up, 300 people in one-- we had to wait a bit, half an hour maybe, [LAUGHS] out in the cold, rubbing ourselves.
And then, quick running behind me, said the commander of-- our future commander, who was there to pick up our group.
And we had to run behind him, which was good-- better than standing in the cold.
And we went run about half a kilometer.
And eventually we arrived to a barrack.
And then they gave us the striped pants and the striped jacket-- no food, mind you, still no food.
But next day, we got a-- a soup with beetroot.
From then on, we had no more cabbage soups.
For a while, they had beetroot soup-- a red soup with pieces of beetroot in it.
So that-- the conditions in-- Mauthausen was flooded by all the other camps got evacuated, you see.
And the whole of Auschwitz was pumped into Mauthausen.
All the camps around Vienna was pushed into Mauthausen.
So a barrack built for 200 people was housing 1,200.
So there was six people to a-- to a bed-- not bed, but a piece of timber, you know.
Six people, six here, six there, six under the beds, six between the beds, and what did the commander do?
They pushed 1,200 people in.
He had to put people into the corridors, between the beds, and the road leading to the so-called toilet.
The toilet was just a dug hole in the ground, at the end of the building.
It was one meter deep, filled with excreta, and urine, whatever.
And so even the road to there was filled with people, lying on top of each other, like wedged in a-- you know, 1,200 people-- better than being outside in the snow.
So I got my place inside.
It was-- people couldn't sleep, they were so tightly packed, legs everywhere in your face.
Half the people had high temperature-- you know, for-- from the Auschwitz people, you know, coming in the open wagon.
It was really very bad.
I was half unconscious.
I had high temperature by that time.
We had soup again next day, had a tiny piece of bread.
And then they started to call out numbers.
We got our number, your numbers.
And they called out numbers to different works, mostly in tunnels, because Austria was full with tunnels by then, where all the German were manufacturing industries, plus the rockets.
They were all built inside the tunnels, because the tunnels the Americans couldn't bomb.
It was the mountain.
They couldn't bomb it.
They couldn't even see where it starts because it was camouflaged with trees in the entrance.
And the tunnel was one kilometer, two kilometer, inside the mountain.
And there was benches, and electricity, and drilling.
And we had machine guns, and airplane parts, and-- and rockets, which fell on England, the V1 and the V2.
So they called out the people to the different jobs, which meant 30, 60 kilometer march through the mountain roads to this tunnel and to that tunnel, to different camps.
So more and more people got called out but my number didn't come up yet.
A lot of my friends already left.
We didn't know where they are going.
But none of them ever returned.
So they went to these tunnels, where-- that's another story-- where the conditions were worse than anything, because the SS men were desperate.
They knew that their life depends on more armaments, rockets, more tanks made, then the longer they can live.
So it's either they live or the Jews live.
So let the Jews die and work faster, and if they die, it doesn't matter.
The next 1,000 is coming in.
So anyhow, going back to the story, I was still there, with high temperature.
Conditions were very, very bad.
Daytime, we had to sit outside in the not so cold.
It was only minus 5.
At nighttime, it was colder.
The nights were horrible.
And then, on about the fifth or sixth night, my temperature got higher and higher.
I was very, very sick.
My whole face was swollen.
I don't know what my sickness was, but probably something to do with freezing.
I was almost frozen dead.
Anyhow, in the middle of the night-- because we were eating-- we were eating snow all day long.
That's the only thing which was in plentiful supply, as regards to food is concerned.
So I had to go to the toilet.
And to go to the toilet was a major effort, even for a healthy person.
And with a person with high temperature, first I have to get out from my bed, with six people lying on me, and around me, and on top of me.
I had to lift up many legs, climb out from the so-called second level of the you know, three-level bed.
And then I-- I stepped down and I stepped on three other people, who were lying there.
So, you bastard, why are you step on me?
I said, sorry, you know.
And then I step on the next one.
To get off him, I had step on the next person.
And so forth, I had to go through-- I had to urinate a bit on some of the people, because I couldn't hold it back any longer.
And so anyhow, I-- I had to do-- this was about-- I had to go about 25 meters, through many bodies, stepping on heads, on stomachs, on legs.
And eventually, with terrific effort, I got out to this half-- it was half outside, half still inside.
It was partially covered, this trench where you're supposed to do your job.
And as I arrived there, it was very icy-- slippery as well.
Anyhow, I fell down, on the edge of the mountain of excreta.
And I passed out.
I passed out.
I know I don't know how long I was passed out.
I was lying there with my hands and legs hanging into the shit.
Eventually, they found me.
And eventually, they-- after robbing me from all my possessions, which was a rag for handkerchief and a spoon-- and that's about all I had.
So they took the spoon and the handkerchief, which I had-- a piece of rag.
And they put me on the stretcher.
And that's where I woke up in the morning, in the commandant's office, commander's office, who-- there were a couple others.
I wasn't the only one-- with half a dozen others.
So next morning, when it came to stand to attention, the SS men counted the people.
So many alive and we'll say a dozen dead.
And then they had six on stretchers, which included me.
And then I was taken to the hospital.
They didn't know what to do with me.
I wasn't quite dead yet.
Dead would have been easier to process.
The dead went right away to the crematorium.

But the people who were not quite dead yet-- they were sent to a special four barracks, which was-- I went back there, the big monument there now, in Mauthausen-- four big round barracks, which was kind of the anti-chamber of death.
They put them there to die.
Those people didn't have to go to work, because they were all near death.
And they couldn't work, certainly.
And they had no gas chamber.
So there was no way to kill them without wasting precious bullets.
So they just put them in there.
And when they were dead, then they took them to the crematorium.
So they took me down there, long steps.
It's about 300, 400 meters from the other camp.
They took me out there and dumped me there.
And I'll come back to that in a minute.
But I just have to mention that on that day-- and the others, who-- who didn't fall down, who were not on stretchers-- their numbers were all called out.
And they all went to Ebensee.
Ebensee was the camp where-- the-- the absolute, the most cruel SS men were there, hitting people with whips, and sticks, and metal bars, and the rifle butt.
They had a steady supply.
They could kill 3,000 a day, because a fresh 3,000 kept coming from the other camps.
So it doesn't matter how many-- [SPEAKING GERMAN].
Carry the cement, and push this, and take the bricks in.
And they were building, extending, the factories inside the tunnel.
They were building work benches, and this and that.
So the conditions were there horrible.
I know because one of my friends came back-- one.
He's in Melbourne.
And he told me that everybody around him died, all his friends, all my friends and his friends, died.
And the situation there was that the camp was about 6 or 8 kilometers from the workplace.
And where they slept, they slept in a tent.
This is just interruption of my story.
But I have to tell his story.
I think it's very important.
Where-- where they slept, in a tent, there was no food whatsoever-- no breakfast, and no bread, and no soup.
So you could only get soup or a piece of bread, if you went the 8 kilometers to the tunnel, to the workplace.
If you worked there your eight or 10 hours, then you had a chance to get some cabbage soup or beetroot soup.
If you stayed at home-- they didn't mind if you stay at home.
If you stayed in your home, in your-- in your tent, then within three days you were dead from hunger.
And the rundown bodies, you-- they just let you sleep there all day long.
And after three, four days, they were dead.
When you were dead, then they-- they throw you out to the other-- to the other-- they built a pyramid of dead bodies later on.
And for a while, the crematorium could handle it.
Later, the crematorium couldn't handle it anymore.
Then they just built big pyramids of dead bodies outside.
It was snow.
So it was good.
Luckily, it was deep frozen, you see?
So summer you couldn't have done it.
But in winter, you could do it.

So he just told me, the way he survived is that he walked back to the tunnel, and standing there, waiting to be put into a bunch of people who carry bags of cement, he just-- he could see a spade in ad against the wall.
So he went up to the spade.
He ran-- he went up to the spade and started to shovel-- shovel, it was, shoveling snow, digging roads through the snow from one tunnel to the other workplace.
And he was digging snow like mad.
And the other people were all taken to work to carry bags of cement inside the tunnel.
They were beaten all day long and killed.
He was digging with the shovel outside.
On the second day, each time the SS officer came past him, he just stood to attention.
And the SS men just said [SPEAKING GERMAN].
And they-- he assume that some SS man gave him his job.
Nobody gave it to him.
He just gave it to himself, to just dig the snow, which is light work.
Just you know, snow is easier than cement.
So next day, they arrive to the work.
He right away stood out.
And they know it, who is the one.
Went to the-- started shoveling the snow.
And there was plenty of snow, luckily for him.
He was doing that for weeks and weeks.
SS men came and go.
He saluted them.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]-- don't stop for me.
So, oh, all right, so he carried on.

And that's how he was, shoveling the snow, until the week when-- when they wanted to drive all the remaining Jews, the new-- when the old ones died, the January, February crop was all dead, of course.
But the new ones arrived in March and April.
And the last 20,000 or so were ordered to go inside the tunnel.
And they dynamited the tunnels, when the Americans approached.
And they wanted to blow up all the Jews inside the tunnel-- you know, close the doors and just blow the whole place up.
But the people refused to go in.
And they said to the commander that if you do that, then you will be hanged himself within a week.
But the Americans are nearly here, so the SS man changed his-- he just disappeared.
He changed his clothing.
He became a farmer.
And then they arrested him as a farmer, a couple of weeks later.
So the last [INAUDIBLE]-- this is my friend's story.
That's how he survived.
He was shoveling snow the whole winter.
And then the Americans liberated him.
If I would have not passed out on that morning, early February, first of all, you had to march 50 miles to get to Ebensee from Mauthausen.
Then you had to work inside the tunnel.
I would have died for sure.
It is 99.9% that I would have died.
So by passing out, by having this terrible high temperature, that saved my life.
But there was more than that to it, because I got to this anti-chamber of death, this hospital block-- four big buildings, but I was in the first one.
And that had a so-called anti-chamber, where you know, just opening the door, that was the first small room.
And then came two big-- two longer rooms.
The first room had an open door.
There was no windows.
The wind blew in.
The snow came into the room itself.
Now there were four beds in that room.
They put me on one of the beds.
They just plunked me down.
And that's it.
They put me on the bed.
And I was lying there with a high temperature.
The next day, the commander come-- came and went.
And I said, can I have a blanket, please?
I ask him at night.
He said, no, no, we have no spare blankets.
You don't need one.
I said, well, I need one.
I'm cold.
Oh, you'll be dead by-- by the morning, anyhow.
We haven't got blankets to waste on people who are almost dead, you see.
And you will be dead by tomorrow morning.
And the morning came.
And then they brought in the hot coffee-- so-called coffee.
Everybody got a you know, helping of coffee.
And I didn't get one.
I said, can I have some of hot drink?
No, I don't-- you don't get one.
You'll be dead today anyhow.
Sometime during the day, you will die.
You don't need coffee.
And then came the soup-- no soup, no blanket.
It went on like that for two days.
And after two days, I again asked him, can I have a blanket, please?
He said, what?
You still alive, he said.
You still alive?
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
I said, I'm sorry.
I'm still here, yeah.
I'm still very cold.
OK, I'll get you a blanket.
I got a blanket.
And after a while, suddenly I see Dr.
Hoffmann come in, the Christian Polish doctor.
He had his doctor's badge.
He was in good condition.
He was elegant.
He had this fresh-- freshly-ironed striped uniform on.
He was OK.
And I ask him, hello, Dr.
Hoffmann.
I'm here.
He said, what are you doing?
Oh, I've got high temperature and I-- I said, could you get me something to eat?
He said, sure, sure, I'll give you something to eat.
And he brought me a soup.
Then he brought me a coffee.
And I-- I said, I could use one more blanket, too.
All right, yeah-- I brought me-- because he was a good friend in Auschwitz.
He brought me a blanket.
So to the amazement of everybody, other people died around me-- the other three beds, they died.
They throwed out their bodies, put new one in, throw them out, put another one in.
I was still there, after three or four days.
And now Dr.
Hoffmann came again, to have his routine checkups, to select out the dead bodies from the alive ones.
And I said-- I said, look, I feel a little better now.
You think you could transfer me from this room into the main room, where-- where they give food from time to time?
Because here I get next to nothing?
He said, I'll see what I can do.
And he came back after three hours.
And, yes, come with me.
And he helped me, and with my blanket, my precious blanket-- and I had a spoon by then again-- and took me to the main room, which was not a holiday place, I can assure you.
There was about 1,000 people in there-- again, six in a bed.
They were very skinny.
But, still, six was a bit too much.

The legs hanging, into-- you know, the usual story, people kicking each other, people lying under the bed, on the bed, top of the bed, people urinating on top of you, too lazy to come down and doing it in the spot, and all trickling down on you.
Anyhow, they put me in there.
That was the middle of February.
And my temperature was gone by then.
But it was terrible undernourishment.
Everybody had sores, full with puss, and all the legs, their arms.
And lying on these wooden beds made it worse.
Because bones were sticking out.
There was no more flesh in their bodies, just bones rubbing against the timber.
So it was-- it got [INAUDIBLE] the sores started to bleed-- not just mine, but everybody, everybody's.
And the puss, infected, especially where the bones tend to stick out, like here, and here, this bone, and that bone.
So it was everybody was full with sores, terribly hungry.
And so the days went on like that.
Just-- I just give you a couple episodes.
I can't tell you day by day, but people dying all the time.
The dead bodies had to be-- the rule was, if somebody dies in your-- from your six, then you throw out the body-- joint effort.
Throw him out on the floor.
And then the collectors come and they pull them out.
At this stage, they-- they didn't bury anybody.
They just pulled them out and accumulated them outside the building.
Both sides, there were mountains of dead bodies going up to the sky-- men, women, together.
Anyhow, inside, one night, I was fighting-- you know, the usual story, everybody shouting at everybody.
Put your leg out of my mouth.
You kick me in the eye.
Can't you watch your leg?
You know, and he's lying there and my leg is in his mouth.
So anyhow, there was some noise.
He said something.
I shouted back at him-- not shouted, but said something to him.
There's terrific noise.
Anyhow, the room commander got fed up.
And he came there with his heavy stick.
And he just hit us a bit, me and him, and all around the general area.
And then he was angry at me.
I mean, I was nearest to him.
So he just pulled me out-- out with you, and with you.
You are trouble makers, you two.
You go outside.
You stand outside the building.
You stand next to that tree.
And don't you move, because if you move, then I beat you to pulp.
So I had to stand outside on the mountaintop, again, you know, like when we arrived-- stand outside in my summer uniform, and stand out there in February-- end of February, at least minus 20.
And I didn't know.
I thought, if I had to stand here all night, I freeze to death.
Another thing, too-- I didn't have any-- I was barefoot.
Barefoot.
And after about two or three hours, he came out again.
OK, you can go back now, but make sure you shut up, and I don't want to hear any sounds.
No-- no naughtiness, you know, behave yourself.
I said, OK, yes, I'll do my best.
I'll be quiet like a mouse.
So that was one little story.

And then the other-- other thing what happened is that of course, people started to lose their teeth because malnutrition, lack of all the vitamins, all the gums got infected and sore, and swollen, full with puss, and the teeth just fell out.
They didn't fully fell out till you pull it out yourself because it was-- they were just in the way, you know?
You just swallowed it, or-- so all the teeth-- it fell out over a six week period, not-- not just in one day.
But every day, you lost one or two teeth.
And the whole mouth was-- which was a big problem.
The gums were badly rotting and full with puss.
And if you managed to get a piece of bread, then it was a major problem.
You had to-- you couldn't swallow it in one.
So you had to break it up with your finger into smaller units, and then just make it wet with your saliva, and somehow swallow it, because you couldn't not eat and rotten gums.
So it was a very difficult situation to be in.
So that's how it was.
And the dead bodies-- more and more people died.
So the stage came that every morning, from the six people, we had to throw out three.
Two or three every morning we had to throw out.
But they quickly filled it up, because new and new transports arrived from Hungary, from Czechoslovakia, from Romania.
People were driven to the-- to the Reich.
And so we see the new transports coming in, relatively fleshy people, who still have-- who had-- who have eaten something the previous few weeks.
And they came in.
And then they died, in turn.
Of course, after four, six weeks, they died from hunger.
But they always got filled up.
So when you throw out three bodies, doesn't mean that you had the place to yourself.
By the evening, it was full up again with-- with thicker and fat-- I mean, relatively speaking, not so starved people as we were.
So that's-- that's how the time went.
We went-- we went through these months, February, March.
The only good thing was that the SS man left his paper with the chief Jewish doctor.
Doctor-- I mean, but he could do two things.
First of all, he left his SS paper there, the [GERMAN], the [GERMAN].
And we could read that the German troops achieved a big victory, holding back the enemy at Vienna, [LAUGHS] and always winning, of course with-- with big defeat on the Russian forces.
We killed several thousand west of Vienna-- east of Vienna, and then west of Vienna, and then next-- next to Breslau, and 100 miles from Berlin.
So we could see what-- what's going on.
And the Americans suffered a terrific defeat on crossing the Rhine.
So we knew that they crossed the Rhine.
And then they had a big defeat at some other place near Frankfurt.
And Germans were always winning, naturally.
There were always big victories.
And the Fuhrer always promised the final surprise weapon, daily.
It was the V1, the V2.
And wait until the V-- that was the atomic bomb.
But he couldn't make it in time.
One more year, and he would have had it.
And so the V1 and V2, already killing thousands of Englishmen, which was true.
And he said, the V3 is coming.
So just a bit of more patience and we'll lick the Americans completely and we'll wipe out England and we'll win for sure.
That's what he said, even on the day when the Russians entered Berlin.
Victory is ours, no doubt about that.
So that we could read the paper.
We could read the SS man's paper.
That was a big benefit of-- of the Jewish doctor.
And the other big benefit was that we had one doctor for 1,200 people.
And every morning, he was holding one hour or two hour surgery hours-- you know, and he had to go to other blocks, too.
Well, anybody with a big rotten-- big-- you know, you got these swollen, rotten things.
So one day, I went up to him.
He just took out his pocket knife.
And he said, look, I can't do everything.
But I'll do this one, that one.
I'll do these four.
And he just cut-- cut into it with his pocket night.
He pushed out the thing.
Then he gets some toilet papers, which he stuck on in the cut here.
He had about four or five cuts he made.
It was good, because he pushed out all the infected parts.

Say, I can't do much with your gums, unfortunately, which was indeed very unfortunate, because the daily bread portion-- see, what happened in April, by that time, all order-- law and order broke down you know, in that part of Austria.
There was no more supplies from-- from Hungary.
Hungary was already in Russian hands.
No more food coming to Mauthausen.
Oh, sorry.
I-- I have to--
